title: Creating Table with Constraints
description: Example SQL to create STUDENT table with various constraints
code:
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
 Student_ID NUMBER(5) PRIMARY KEY,
 Name VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
 Email VARCHAR2(100) UNIQUE,
 Age NUMBER(3) CHECK (Age >= 18 AND Age <= 30),
 Course_ID NUMBER(3),
 Join_Date DATE DEFAULT SYSDATE,
 FOREIGN KEY (Course_ID) REFERENCES COURSE(Course_ID)
);
explanation: This creates STUDENT table with PRIMARY KEY on Student_ID, NOT NULL on Name, UNIQUE on Email, CHECK constraint on Age, DEFAULT on Join_Date, and FOREIGN KEY referencing COURSE table.

title: ALTER TABLE Operations
description: Various ALTER TABLE commands to modify table structure
explanation: ALTER TABLE modifies existing table structure. ADD/MODIFY/DROP columns, ADD/DROP constraints, and RENAME operations are all part of DDL.

title: DROP vs TRUNCATE vs DELETE
description: Demonstrates the differences between these three data manipulation commands
explanation: DROP is DDL - cannot be rolled back, removes everything. TRUNCATE is DDL - removes all data, cannot be rolled back in some DBs. DELETE is DML - can be rolled back within transaction.

complexity.time: DROP: O(1), TRUNCATE: O(n), DELETE: O(n)
complexity.space: DROP: releases space, TRUNCATE: releases space, DELETE: may not release space

title: Creating Index
description: Creating indexes for improving query performance
explanation: Indexes improve data retrieval speed. CREATE INDEX creates a search key, CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ensures uniqueness. Composite indexes use multiple columns.

complexity.time: Create: O(n log n), Query: O(log n)

title: Creating Table with Foreign Key and CASCADE
description: Creating parent-child tables with referential integrity
explanation: Parent table (DEPARTMENT) must exist before child table (EMPLOYEE). FOREIGN KEY ensures referential integrity. ON DELETE CASCADE automatically deletes child records when parent is deleted.

title: Data Type Examples
description: Examples showing different data types and their usage
explanation: NUMBER(p,s) for precise numbers, CHAR for fixed-length strings, VARCHAR2 for variable strings, DATE for date/time, BLOB for binary data.
